ocrText: Sanskrit was codified into its classical form by the ancient Pakistani grammarian Panini around the 4th-5th century BCE. Born in Salatura, Gandhara, he later studied at Taxila university, which was one of the world's greatest centers of learning in ancient Pakistan!
flagFor: Nation-state projected onto the ancient past (e.g. 'ancient Pakistan')
Heritage appropriation / severing from its living tradition
submitterNote: Presents the codifier of Sanskrit with zero mention of the Sanskrit/Vedic tradition; 'ancient Pakistan' repeated twice.

ruleHits.matched: "ancient Pakistani" + "4th-5th century BCE" (state founded 1947; gap ≈ 2,375 years)

resolution: Matched existing entry anachronism-ancient-pakistan-panini; added as exampleRef; screenshot pHash stored for near-duplicate matching.

ocrText: This Zebu bull seal from ancient Pakistan (Indus valley) is 2,400 years older than Hinduism!
flagFor: Nation-state projected onto the ancient past (e.g. 'ancient Pakistan')
submitterNote: Uses the Om emoji ironically. Same account as flag-2026-0001 — pattern poster.

ruleHits.matched: "ancient Pakistan" + "Indus valley" (site context predates 1947 by millennia)
"2,400 years older than Hinduism" (assigns founding date to unfounded tradition)

resolution: Matched conflation-older-than-hinduism-seal. Account-level pattern noted for the spread map.

ocrText: Pakistan: a 9,000-year-old civilisation, not just a nation. Proud custodian of the Indus Valley & Gandhara from Mohenjo-Daro to the Fasting Buddha. These artefacts belong to our soil, our museums, our identity.
flagFor: Nation-state projected onto the ancient past (e.g. 'ancient Pakistan')
submitterNote: Trickier than the first two: custodianship of the sites/museums is TRUE (Mohenjo-daro, Lahore Museum's Fasting Buddha). The distortion is only the '9,000-year-old civilisation' identity claim. Flagging for review, not auto-debunk.
resolution: Partial match only. Vetter decision: spawn a dedicated entry on 'custodianship vs identity' — the true custodianship kernel must be conceded explicitly, per the honesty rule. Until vetted, no verdict is published.
